content.full_text:
Document No.

Date: August 2, 1990
File No.:

Memorandum
California Public Employees' Retirement System

To: Scott Hallabrim
Acting General Counsel
Legal Department
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

From: Board of Administration
Lincoln Plaza, 400 P Street
Sacramento, CA

Subject:

Request for Written Advice (Gov. Code sec. 83114(b) and Cal.
Code Regs., tit. 2, sec. 18329(b).)

This is a request by the Public Employees' Retirement System
(PERS) for written advice pursuant to Government Code section
83114(b) and title 2, California Code of Regulations, section
18329(b). A statement in compliance with Commission Regulation
19329(2)(A) is attached as Appendix A.

Issue A:

Do PERS Board Members, officers or employees manage "public
investments" within the meaning of the term as used in
Government Code section 87200?

Effective January 1, 1990, Government Code section 87200 was
amended to include ". . . other public officials who manage
public investments. . . ." (Emphasis added.) A March 22, 1990
memorandum from Blanca Breeze and Margaret Ellison of the FPPC
Legal Office to the FPPC Chairman and Commissioners states as
follows:

"We construe the term 'public investments' to
include investments made with public moneys. 'Public
moneys' includes all bonds and evidence of
indebtedness, and all moneys belonging to the state,
or bonds, and evidences of indebtedness received or
held by state, county, district, city, town or public
agency officers in their official capacity. (Penal
Code Section 426.) Therefore, the scope of the
amendment to Section 87200 covers all public
agencies, including special districts." (Emphasis
added.) (P. 2 of memo.)

PERS—ADM—76 (Rev. 8/86)

Scott Hallibrim                    2                    August 2, 1990


The monies which are held and invested by PERS are not public
moneys or part of the general fund, but rather are trust funds
pursuant to both the California Constitution and the Government
Code.

California Constitution article XVI, section 17(a) states as
follows:

     "(a) The assets of a public pension or
retirement system are trust funds and shall be held
for the exclusive purposes of providing benefits to
participants in the pension or retirement system and
their beneficiaries and defraying reasonable expenses
of administering the system."

Government Code section 20200 provides as follows in pertinent
part:

     ". . . The Public Employees' Retirement Fund is
a trust fund created, and administered in accordance
with the provisions of this part, solely for the
benefit of the members and retired members of the
System and their survivors and beneficiaries."

PERS is funded by (1) employer contributions and (2) employee
contributions, and (3) the interest thereon.  PERS does not
borrow money from the State and is not indebted to the State.
Hence, it would not appear that any PERS officials or employees
including, but not limited to, PERS Board Members, the Chief
Executive Officer, Assistant Executive Staff, the Investment
Manager or any investment staff member would fall within the
definition of a "public official who manage[s] public
investments. . . ."  Therefore, it would appear that no PERS
Board Member, officer or employee should be subject to the
broad reporting requirements of Government Code section 87200.

If you disagree with PERS' conclusion in this regard, please
provide us with an explanation which supports your conclusions.

Issue B:

Are individuals who hold the positions discussed below, "public
officials who manage public investments" within the meaning of
Government Code section 87200?

If the FPPC determines that PERS does administer "public
investments" as the term is used in Government Code section
87200, PERS requests the FPPC's opinion as to whether
individuals holding the positions described in the contracts
and job duty statements, which are attached as Appendix B, are

Scott Hallibrim                3                August 2, 1990


subject to the broad reporting requirements of Government Code
section 87200.¹

We have attached the most current PERS organizational chart to
assist in your determinations in this regard.  (Appendix C.)

By way of background, Government Code section 20201 gives the
PERS Board "exclusive control of the administration and
investment of the Retirement Fund."  Government Code section
20205.6 states that "the board has exclusive control of the
investment of the retirement fund."

The Chief Executive Officer of PERS "monitors the work of
investment staff" and "directs . . . investments." (See
attached duty statement in Appendix B.) The Chief Investment
Officer (also known as the Investment Manager) is a contract
position. This position reports directly to the Chief
Executive Officer. The Assistant Executive Officer who is in
charge of investment operations reports to the Chief Executive
Officer. While there are currently four Assistant Executive
Officers, it is PERS' position that only the Assistant
Executive Officer over investment operations makes investment
decisions. Other internal investment staff report to their
various supervisors as set forth on their duty statements.
(See Appendix B.) It is PERS' position that these internal
staff members should remain designated employees on PERS
Conflict of Interest Code, because their work is subject to
several levels of review and supervision.

Neither the General Counsel nor the Assistant General Counsel
make decisions to buy or sell investments. Under the direction
of the Board, individuals holding these positions prepare and
present shareholder proposals to corporations in which PERS is
a shareholder, oversee the work of outside investment
consultants, and represent and/or oversee representation of
PERS in legal proceedings involving companies in which PERS
invests funds.




_______________
¹ These positions are all presently designated in PERS'
agency Conflict of Interest Code.

Scott Hallibrim                    4                    August 2, 1990


Issue C:

Do Government Code sections 89503, 89505 and 89506 apply to
individuals (other than members of state boards and
commissions) who are subject to the broad reporting
requirements of Government Code section 87200?

Government Code sections 89503, 89505 and 89506 expressly apply
to members of state boards or commissions and "designated
employees."

Pursuant to Government Code section 82019(d), the term
"designated employee" does not include ". . . any public
official specified in Section 87200. . . ."

Thus, to the extent that the FPPC determines that certain PERS
officers or employees are subject to the broad reporting
requirements of Government Code section 87200, it is PERS'
position that these officers and employees are not designated
employees, and hence, not subject to the prohibitions and
limits of Government Code section 89503, 89505 or 89506.
Further, it is PERS' position that the FPPC lacks any
authority to promulgate regulations which would include
individuals specified in Government Code section 87200, within
the purview of sections 89503-89506.

PERS is aware of Government Code section 83112 which authorizes
the Fair Political Practices Commission to "adopt, amend and
rescind rules and regulations to carry out the purposes and
provisions of this title. . . ." However, PERS is also aware
that this statute further expressly states that:

     "These rules and regulations [adopted by the
FPPC] . . . shall be consistent with this title and
other applicable law."

PERS further notes the well-settled legal principle that courts
not only may, but have an obligation to, strike down
administrative regulations which alter, amend, enlarge, or
impair the scope of a statute. (Handlery v. Franchise Tax
Board (1972) 26 Cal.App.3d 970, 981.)

Scott Hallibrim                5                    August 2, 1990


Issue D:

How does the passage of Government Code sections 89506 and
89504 affect title 2, California Code of Regulations, section
18728?

Commission Regulation 18728 states as follows in part:

     "(a) As used in this section, 'honorarium' means
a payment for speaking at any event, participating in
a panel or seminar or engaging in any similar
activity. For purposes of this section, free
admission, food, beverages and similar nominal
benefits provided to a filer at an event at which he
or she speaks, participates in a panel or seminar or
performs a similar service, and reimbursement or
advance for actual intrastate travel and for
necessary accommodations provided directly in
connection with the event are not payments and need
not be reported by the filer. . . . (Emphasis
added.)

Several PERS Board Members who are subject to Government Code
sections 89506 and 89504 are attempting to plan trips in 1991.
Thus, PERS needs a response to this issue at your earliest
opportunity.

Issue E:

What changes to PERS Conflict of Interest Code are required by
Government Code sections 89503(c) and 89595(c)?

Government Code section 89503 imposes restrictions and
prohibitions on the receipt of honorarium. Section 89505
imposes restrictions and prohibitions on the receipt of gifts.
Both sections require every state agency to amend its Conflict
of Interest Code section no later than December 31, 1991 to
comply with these new provisions of law.

In regard to the required amendments to the Conflict of
Interest Codes of state agencies, does the FPPC have any
specific language which it suggests using? If not, would the
following language be acceptable:

Scott Hallibrim                    6                    August 2, 1990


"PROHIBITIONS AND RESTRICTIONS APPLICABLE
TO ALL DISCLOSURE CATEGORIES

"No Board member or designated employee shall accept:

"1.  An honorarium from any source, if the member or employee
would be required to report the receipt of income or gifts
from that source on his or her Statement of Economic
Interests. An honorarium is any payment in consideration
for any speech given, article published, or attendance at
any public or private conference, convention, meeting,
meal or like gathering.

"2.  Gifts with a total value of more than two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) in a calendar year from any single source,
if the member or employee would be required to report the
receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her
Statement of Economic Interests."

This language would be placed on page 4 of the Appendix to
PERS' Code, following the disclosure categories. (PERS'
current Code, is attached for your convenience as Appendix D.)

If our proposed language does not satisfy the requirements of
Government Code sections 89503(c) and 89505(c), please provide
us with some alternative language which PERS may use.

Issue F:

When does the 12 month period set forth in Government Code
section 87103(e) begin to run for disqualification purposes?

Government Code section 87103 states as follows:

"An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on the official or a member of
his or her immediate family or on:

". . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

"(e) any donor of, or any intermediary or agent
for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or premised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made. . . ." (Emphasis added.)

Scott Hallibrim                 7                      August 2, 1990


Does one look to the date the decision is to be made and then
count back for 12 months? For example, Assume that Company X
gives a public official a gift worth $50 on September 1, 1989;
another gift worth $150 on July 1, 1990; another gift worth
$100 on August 1, 1990, and another gift worth $100 on
December 31, 1990. Could the public official in this example
have participated in a decision which affected Company X, on
December 1, 1990, because he had not received gifts aggregating
$250 in the 12 months prior to December 1, 1990?

Issue G:

Reporting Business Positions.

Are individuals who are subject to the broad reporting
requirements of Government Code sections 87200 and 87203
required to report business positions which foreseeably may be
affected materially by any decision made or participated in by
the designated employee by virtue of his or her position?

If so, what is the legal authority for this reporting
requirement and on which form would this interest be reported?
If not, what is the rationale for requiring designated
employees to report such positions, but not requiring
individuals who report under Government Code section 87200 to
report such positions?

Issue H:

Disqualifications Issues.

Are the statutes and regulations which govern disqualification,
the same for public officials who are subject to the broad
reporting requirements of Government Code section 87200, as
they are for designated employees under PERS' Conflict of
Interest Code? If not, please provide us with an explanation
of how and when individuals subject to the broad disclosure
requirements must disqualify themselves, and of how and when
designated employees under PERS' Code must disqualify themselves.

Thank you for your anticipated response to this request for
legal advice. If you need further information, please contact
Margaret Hoehn of this office at 326-3666 at your convenience.

[signature]

RICHARD H. KOPPES
General Counsel

RHK:jks

Attachments

sections.question: Issue A: Do PERS Board Members, officers or employees manage "public investments" within the meaning of the term as used in Government code section 87200? Issue B: Are individuals who hold the positions discussed below, "public officials who manage public investments" within the meaning of Government Code section 87200? Issue C: Do Government Code sections 89503, 89505 and 89506 apply to individuals (other than members of state boards and commissions) who are subject to the broad reporting requirements of Government Code section 87200? Issue D: How does the passage of Government Code sections 89506 and La728? Issue E: What changes to PERS Conflict of Interest Code are required by Government Code sections 89503(c) and 89595(c)? Issue F: When does the 12 month period set forth in Government Code section 87103(e) begin to run for disqualification purposes? Issue G: Reporting Business Positions.
sections.question_synthetic: PERS requests FPPC written advice on seven issues regarding whether PERS board members, officers, and employees are subject to Government Code sections 87200, 89503-89506 and related provisions, how to amend the PERS Conflict of Interest Code, and clarification on the 12-month lookback period for gift aggregation in disqualification analysis.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from PERS to FPPC, not an FPPC response. Contains seven distinct issues (A-G) with detailed factual background and legal arguments. Document appears truncated at Issue G. No FPPC conclusion/advice sections present. High confidence in identification as incoming request; moderate confidence due to truncation and complex multi-issue format.

Issue B: Are individuals who hold the positions discussed below, "public officials who manage public investments" within the meaning of Government Code section 87200? Issue C: Do Government Code sections 89503, 89505 and 89506 apply to individuals (other than members of state boards and commissions) who are subject to the broad reporting requirements of Government Code section 87200? Issue D: How does the passage of Government Code sections 89506 and La728? Issue E: What changes to PERS Conflict of Interest Code are required by Government Code sections 89503(c) and 89595(c)? Issue F: When does the 12 month period set forth in Government Code section 87103(e) begin to run for disqualification purposes? Issue G: Reporting Business Positions.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from California Public Employees' Retirement System (PERS) seeking FPPC written advice on multiple conflict of interest law questions, including whether PERS officials manage "public investments" under Government Code section 87200, which PERS positions are subject to reporting requirements, required amendments to the PERS Conflict of Interest Code, and clarification on gift aggregation timing rules.
